coredata.dc:title: Formation of graphene quantum dots by ball-milling technique using carbon nanocapsules and sodium carbonate
coredata.dc:creator: Youh, Meng-Jey
Jiang, Min-Yu
Chung, Meng-Chih
Tai, Hung-Chun
Li, Yuan-Yao
coredata.dc:description: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a wide range of applications in energy, optoelectronics, and bio-nanotechnology. However, their high cost is a barrier for commercial application. Here, we report a cost-effective ball-milling method for producing GQDs using a mixture of carbon nanocapsules (20 nm) as the carbon source and sodium carbonate as the intercalation agent. The results show that the proposed exfoliation method produces GQDs with diameters of 1.0–4.0 nm and a very small amount of grafted functional groups that exhibit blue light emission under ultraviolet irradiation. The quantum yield is about 5%. The proposed method has great potential for the production of GQDs using low-cost raw materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dot
Ball-milling
Carbon nanocapsule
Sodium carbonate

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Photograph of GQDs in deionized water, (b) TEM image of GQDs (inset shows the SAED pattern), (c) particle size distribution, (d) EDS spectrum of GQDs, (e) HRTEM image of GQDs, and (f) HRTEM image of a single GQD (inset shows the SAED pattern obtained using a nanobeam).
(a) UV–visible spectrum (inset photographs of GQD solution taken under visible light (left) and UV light (right), (b) PL spectra, (c) PLE spectrum of GQDs.

head.ce:title: Formation of graphene quantum dots by ball-milling technique using carbon nanocapsules and sodium carbonate

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Ming Chi University of Technology, New Taipei 24301, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Ming Chi University of Technology, New Taipei 24301, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Chung Cheng University, Chia-Yi 62102, Taiwan
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Chung Cheng University, Chia-Yi 62102, Taiwan

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Advanced Institute of Manufacturing with High-Tech Innovations, National Chung Cheng University, Chia-Yi 62102, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Advanced Institute of Manufacturing with High-Tech Innovations

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Advanced Institute of Manufacturing with High-Tech Innovations, National Chung Cheng University, Chia-Yi 62102, Taiwan

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemical Engineering, National Chung Cheng University, Chia-Yi 62102, Taiwan.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) have a wide range of applications in energy, optoelectronics, and bio-nanotechnology. However, their high cost is a barrier for commercial application. Here, we report a cost-effective ball-milling method for producing GQDs using a mixture of carbon nanocapsules (20 nm) as the carbon source and sodium carbonate as the intercalation agent. The results show that the proposed exfoliation method produces GQDs with diameters of 1.0–4.0 nm and a very small amount of grafted functional groups that exhibit blue light emission under ultraviolet irradiation. The quantum yield is about 5%. The proposed method has great potential for the production of GQDs using low-cost raw materials.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphene, a two-dimensional sheet of sp2-hybridized carbon, has excellent mechanical [1], electronic [2,3], and thermal [4] properties. Research on the synthesis of graphene has been conducted ever since Geim and Novoselov used Scotch tape to produce graphene [5]. Graphene is not suitable for applications in photonics and optoelectronics [6] because it is regarded as a zero-band-gap semiconductor with an infinite Bohr radius [7,8]. Zero-dimensional graphene quantum dots (GQDs) are carbon nanocrystals with sizes below 10 nm [9]. They have attracted considerable attention because of their unique optical and electronic properties, which are mainly due to the edge effect and quantum confinement effect [10–13]. GQDs have many advantages, including high photostability against photobleaching [14,15], low-toxicity [16], excellent biocompatibility [17], ease of surface modification, and a tunable band gap [18,19], which make them a promising material for photovoltaic devices [20] and bio-imaging applications [21,22].

Various synthetic methods have been developed for preparing luminescent carbon/graphene quantum dots. They can be classified into top-down and bottom-up methods [23]. Generally, top-down methods directly reduce carbon-based materials, such as carbon black [24], carbon nanotube [25], graphene oxide [26], and activated carbon [23], to a quantum size through hydrothermal methods [24], electrochemical methods [27], or laser ablation [28]. In contrast, bottom-up methods build nanodots from small molecules using pyrolysis [29], microwave-assisted hydrothermal [30], or combustion [31] methods. Both top-down and bottom-up methods have drawbacks. For instance, they use strong concentrated acid or expensive equipment, have a low production rate, or are time-consuming. Furthermore, the use of corrosive agents may damage the graphitic structures of the carbon precursor [32,33] and introduce a large number of defects and functional groups [34]. Thus, it is important to develop a facile, environmentally friendly, efficient, and simple method for the production of GQDs [24,35].

In high-energy ball-milling, which is widely used in the synthesis of nanomaterials [36], moving zirconium balls transfer their kinetic energy to the milled material [37]. The objective of the milling is to reduce the particle size and produce nanomaterials on a large scale with nanosized grains. In addition, this technique can be used to create many desired functional groups on the material surface. For instance, GQDs with numerous oxygen-containing functional groups can be derived from activated carbon powder using potassium hydroxide [23].

Herein, we demonstrate a scalable method for producing GQDs by ball-milling with a mixture of carbon nanocapsules (CNCs) [38] and sodium carbonate, followed by dialysis purification to remove residual ions. The as-prepared GQDs show blue photoluminescence, a narrow size distribution, and a very small amount of functional groups.

CNCs (diameter: 20–30 nm), hollow cores wrapped with graphene layers, were obtained using a previously reported acetylene flame method [38]. Sodium carbonate was purchased from J. T. Baker. Quinine hemisulfate salt monohydrate (≥90%) was purchased from SIGMA. A dialysis bag with a molecular weight cut-off of 3500 Da was obtained from Orange Scientific. All the reagents used were analytical grade and used without further purification.

GQDs were prepared as follows. A mixture of the CNCs and sodium carbonate (Na2CO3) with various mass ratios of CNC/Na2CO3 (e.g., 1.0, 0.5, 0.2, and 0.1) and zirconia balls with a diameter of 300 µm were loaded in a 125-ml grinding jar. After the ball-milling (Retsch PM 100) process at 500 rpm for 4 h, deionized water was added into the mixture to form a turbid dispersion. After the process of spontaneous settlement, the supernatant was collected and then dialyzed with the dialysis bag for 24 h (retained molecular weight: 3500 Da) to obtain GQDs. From experiments, we found that the optimal mass ratios of CNC/Na2CO3 was 0.2.

Transmission electron microscopy (TEM, FEI E.O Tecnai F20 G2 MAT S-TWIN Field Emission Gun at 200 kV) with energy-dispersive X-ray spectrometry (EDS) was employed for the observation of the material morphology. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, PHI Hybrid Quantera) was conducted to observe the chemical binding states of the GQDs and possible functional groups. The absorbance and fluorescence spectra of GQDs were analyzed with a Shimadzu UV-2600 spectrophotometer (365 nm) and a Hitachi F4500 photoluminescence (PL), respectively. The quantum yield of the GQDs was calculated using the results of photoluminescence (PL) based on the spectrum of quinine sulfate.

The obtained GQD dispersions were stable and did not exhibit any precipitation or agglomeration after 6 months, as shown in Fig. 1 (a). Fig. 1(b) shows a TEM image of the obtained GQDs. The selected area electron diffraction (SAED) pattern in the inset of Fig. 1(b) indicates a collection of GQD crystals with different orientations. Fig. 1(c) demonstrates that the particle size distribution of the GQDs in the solvent was mainly in the range of 1.0–4.0 nm according to the image analysis of the TEM image in Fig. 1(b). The EDS results in Fig. 1(d) reveal that C and O were the main elements on the copper grids and that no Ca was detected, indicating good separation of the ions and GQDs by the dialysis method. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image in Fig. 1(e) demonstrates that the in-plane lattice spacing was approximately 0.21 nm, which is close to the (001) spacing of a graphitic structure. Fig. 1(f) shows an image of a single GQD and the inset shows its corresponding SAED pattern obtained using a nanobeam. The spot pattern clearly shows that the GQD has good crystallinity.

Fig. 2 shows the C 1s XPS spectrum of the GQDs, which was fitted by three peaks, namely at 284.6 eV (graphitic carbons, sp2), 286.0 eV (alcoholic carbons, CO), and 288.5 eV (carboxyl carbons, COOH). The percentages of CC, CO, and COOH bonds were calculated as 90.3%, 3.62%, and 6.08%, respectively, according to the integration area of the fitting curve of the three peaks. The GQDs were 91.8% carbon and 8.2% oxygen. A comparison to the C 1s XPS spectrum of GQDs from studies that used Hummer’s method indicated that the intensities of the peaks representing the functional groups in our GQDs were relatively low [39], which indicates that a very small amount of the functional groups were grafted in the GQDs. Fig. 3 (a) shows the ultraviolet (UV)-visible absorption spectrum of the GQDs. Generally, graphene has a single π-π* transition of the CC bond at 270 nm; however, for our GQDs, absorbance peaks were observed at 230 and 300 nm, which are attributed to the blue-shifted π-π* transition of aromatic sp2 domains and the n-π* transition of CO bonds, respectively [32,35,40]. GQDs with blue photoluminescence emission under 365-nm UV light irradiation are shown in the inset of Fig. 3(b). The PL spectra of the GQDs were systematically investigated by tuning the excitation wavelength (λex) from 280 to 540 nm in intervals of 20 nm. In Fig. 3(b), when λex is in the range of 280–360 nm, the PL emission peaks are broad and centered at a wavelength of 440 nm. The intensity of the peaks (λem) increased with increasing λex from 280 to 320 nm and then decreased. The maximum intensity of the peaks was reached at λex = 320 nm. When λem was increased to 380 nm or more, the GQDs exhibited excitation-dependent PL behavior. The intensities decreased rapidly and the emission peaks shifted to a longer wavelength region (i.e., they were red-shifted) [36]. The red-shifting may be associated with the degree of oxidation and the types of surface oxygen-containing groups [41].

The quantum yield of the GQDs was obtained from the analysis of UV–visible spectra using quinine sulfate as the reference compound [24]. The calculated quantum yield of the GQDs was about 5%, which is similar to the results from other studies that used physical exfoliation methods [42,43]. It is believed that the PL mechanism of GQDs is the radiative recombination of surface-confined electrons and holes [41]. The low quantum yield might have been caused by the relatively low amount of functional groups and defects in the GQDs, and thus intrinsic-state emission, not defect-state emission, was dominant [44].

Fig. 3(c) shows the photoluminescence excitation (PLE) spectrum of the GQDs at an emission wavelength of 440 nm. As can be seen, two peaks appear at around 260 nm (4.75 eV) and 320 nm (3.95 eV); they are attributed to π-π* transitions (highest occupied molecular orbital to lowest unoccupied molecular orbital) and σ-π* transitions, respectively. This result is in good agreement with that reported by Hwang et al. [34]. Regarding the formation mechanism of the GQDs using the ball-milling technique, we think that Na2CO3 may act as an intercalating agent for CNCs and that the combination of Na2CO3 and ball-milling played an important role in the formation of the GQDs. A 20-nm CNC is composed of small pieces of graphene sheets wrapped together to form a nanoparticle. During the ball-milling process, Na2CO3 intercalated into graphene sheets due to the thermal expansion of the graphene layers by the high-energy collisions of the zirconia balls. The graphene sheets were then ripped and stripped off from the CNCs. The GQDs thus formed.

We developed a ball-milling method for producing GQDs with low-cost carbon nanocapsules and sodium carbonate. The results show that the produced GQDs have a narrow size distribution (1.0–4.0 nm) and blue light emission under UV irradiation (365 nm). UV–visible and PL spectra indicate that the maximum intensity of the emission peak was at 440 nm with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm. The produced GQDs with a narrow size distribution and a very small amount of functional groups can be used for unique applications.

Meng-Jey Youh: Supervision, Project administration. Min-Yu Jiang: Conceptualization, Investigation, Methodology, Resources, Writing - original draft, Data curation, Visualization. Meng-Chih Chung: Data curation, Investigation, Writing - review & editing, Validation, Resources, Visualization. Hung-Chun Tai: Conceptualization, Investigation. Yuan-Yao Li: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Resources, Writing - review & editing, Supervision, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors would like to thank the Ministry of Science and Technology (MOST), Taiwan, for supporting this research under grant MOST 105-2221-E-194-053-MY3.
The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.inoche.2020.108061.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Measurement of the elastic properties and intrinsic strength of monolayer graphene

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Lee, X.D. Wei, J.W. Kysar, J. Hone, Measurement of the elastic properties and intrinsic strength of monolayer graphene, Science 321(5887) (2008) 385-388.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J.H. Chen, C. Jang, S.D. Xiao, M. Ishigami, M.S. Fuhrer, Intrinsic and extrinsic performance limits of graphene devices on SiO2, Nature Nanotechnology 3(4) (2008) 206-209.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: A.A. Balandin, Thermal properties of graphene and nanostructured carbon materials, Nature Materials 10(8) (2011) 569-581.

L.S. Li, X. Yan, Colloidal Graphene Quantum Dots, Journal of Physical Chemistry Letters 1(17) (2010) 2572-2576.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Surface sensitive photoluminescence of carbon nanodots: coupling between the carbonyl group and π-electron system

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Liu, L. Bao, M. Yang, S. Zhang, M. Zhou, B. Tang, B. Wang, Y. Liu, Z.-L. Zhang, B.J.T.j.o.p.c.l. Zhang, Surface Sensitive Photoluminescence of Carbon Nanodots: Coupling between the Carbonyl Group and π-Electron System, The journal of physical chemistry letters 10(13) (2019) 3621-3629.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bioimaging based on fluorescent carbon dots

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Y.B. Song, S.J. Zhu, B. Yang, Bioimaging based on fluorescent carbon dots, Rsc Advances 4(52) (2014) 27184-27200.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A facile and green method towards coal-based fluorescent carbon dots with photocatalytic activity

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: S. Hu, Z. Wei, Q. Chang, A. Trinchi, J.J.A.S.S. Yang, A facile and green method towards coal-based fluorescent carbon dots with photocatalytic activity, 378 (2016) 402-407.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Lu, Y. Zhu, C. Shi, Y.T.J.C. Pei, Large-scale synthesis of defect-selective graphene quantum dots by ultrasonic-assisted liquid-phase exfoliation, 109 (2016) 373-383.
